ANT 104 · Coevolution

Coevolution of Humans and Dogs: From Hunting and Gathering to Farming

Name: Hammy Siddiqui

Date: June 9, 2026

Class: ANT 104 - Intro to Bio Anthro

Professor: Dr. Alexandra Sumner

I chose this topic because I've always known about and been disheartened by the cruel dog breeding humans have done. I know that since the Victorian era, humans have been selectively breeding dogs not for functionality or ability, but for competitions, appearance, and luxury. Many of these dogs have eventually developed severe mobility and breathing issues, like pugs. From recent knowledge I learned from social media, dachshunds were bred before the Victorian era for a specific and functional reason. Their long bodies and small statures were selectively bred to have the perfect shape to hunt badgers in their own tunnels. Learning this and reading the topic list, I immediately thought of how the era-defining change from hunting to farming affected dogs and humans. I had a couple of questions leading the start of my research:

Research Questions

  • When did humans start domesticating dogs?
  • Why did humans start domesticating dogs?
  • What was the relationship between dogs and humans?
  • Did the switch from hunting and gathering to farming affect the way humans selectively bred dogs in any biological ways?

Research Findings

Quotes researchers said and agreed on:

"All zooarchaeologists support the contention that dogs were not only the first domestic animal, but that the appearance of dogs significantly predates the origins of domestic plants and early agriculture"

(Larson 1)

"Dogs were the first domestic animal, but little is known about their population history and to what extent it was linked to humans. We sequenced 27 ancient dog genomes and found that all dogs share a common ancestry distinct from present-day wolves"

(Bergström 557)

"Dogs were the only domestic species present in both Europe and the Near East prior to the Neolithic"

(Ollivier 1)

"Understanding when and where our relationship with dogs began is crucial to comprehending the transition of humans from hunter-gatherers to farmers"

(Larson 1)
